#TODO: this discusses a potential strategy for trading on the signals generated by the models. It comes from a claude chat on 5/17. This file is to be deleted once trade.py is finished.


This is a fundamentally a signal-processing and control problem dressed up as a trading problem. You have a noisy, fast-updating estimate of a quantity (next-hour return per stock), and you need to convert that into position decisions while managing the tension between responsiveness and stability. Let me walk through how I'd think about it.

## The core tension

Your robot's predictions have two components mixed together: genuine signal (real information about where a stock is heading) and noise (the robot's mistakes, which jitter around every 3 minutes). If you trade on every twitch, you're mostly trading on noise, and you bleed money through transaction costs and whipsaw — buying high, selling low, repeatedly. If you smooth too aggressively, you lag the real moves and arrive at the party after it's over. Everything below is about finding the right place on that spectrum, and ideally adapting where you sit on it depending on conditions.

## Smoothing the signal

The first thing I would not do is feed raw predictions into the portfolio. I'd treat the incoming stream of predictions for each stock as a time series to be filtered.

A reasonable starting point is an exponentially weighted moving average of the predictions. Each new prediction updates a running estimate, where the decay parameter controls how much memory you keep. A slow decay means you're very stable but laggy; a fast decay means responsive but noisy. The EWMA is attractive because it's cheap, online, and naturally weights recent information more.

But a fixed EWMA is crude because the right amount of smoothing depends on how much the robot is contradicting itself. This is where a Kalman-filter framing is more principled. Model each stock's "true" expected return as a hidden state that evolves over time, and treat each robot prediction as a noisy measurement of that state. The filter then optimally blends your prior estimate with each new measurement, weighting by their relative uncertainties. The key practical benefit: you can estimate the robot's measurement noise empirically — by looking at how much its successive predictions disagree — and let the filter automatically smooth more when the robot is being erratic and react faster when its predictions are stable and consistent. That adaptivity is exactly what you want given the prompt's note that "sometimes his predictions change a lot."

A useful refinement is to track not just the smoothed level of the prediction but its trend or momentum — the rate of change of the filtered signal. Riding short waves means you care about the derivative: a stock whose predicted return is rising over the last several updates is a stock entering a wave. This pushes me toward something like a local-level-plus-trend state-space model rather than a pure level filter.

## From signal to conviction to position

Once you have a filtered prediction per stock, don't map it directly to a target weight in a linear way. I'd convert it into a conviction score that accounts for uncertainty. A predicted return is only actionable relative to how confident you are in it — the filter already gives you an error variance, so something analogous to an information ratio (smoothed prediction divided by its estimated standard error) is a better quantity to rank and size on than the raw prediction. Cross-sectionally, you can rank or z-score these across the 500 stocks so your portfolio is responding to relative attractiveness, not absolute prediction levels, which insulates you from regimes where the robot is uniformly too optimistic or pessimistic.

Target positions should be a smooth, saturating function of conviction, not a step function. You want diminishing position size as conviction grows (don't go all-in on the single strongest signal) and you want zero or small positions in the broad middle band where signals are weak. A soft sigmoid-like or clipped-linear mapping works.

## The hysteresis band — the heart of the strategy

This is the most important mechanism for the responsiveness/stability tradeoff, and it's distinct from signal smoothing. Even with a perfectly smoothed signal, if your rule is "hold the top N stocks," a stock hovering at the N/N+1 boundary will be churned in and out constantly. The fix is hysteresis: use two thresholds, not one.

Concretely, you enter a position only when conviction exceeds a high threshold, but you don't exit until conviction falls below a distinctly lower threshold. The gap between entry and exit thresholds creates a "sticky" zone where, once you hold something, you keep holding it through minor signal fluctuations. This directly implements "don't be too quick to react" while still letting you exit decisively when the signal genuinely deteriorates. The same idea applies to position sizing: define a no-trade band around your current position so you only rebalance toward the new target when the gap between current and desired holdings exceeds some threshold. Small desired adjustments are ignored; only meaningful ones trigger trades.

This no-trade band is also where you naturally incorporate transaction costs even though your trades are "informed only by predictions" — the band's width should scale with expected costs, because a trade is only worth doing if the expected improvement from rebalancing exceeds the cost of executing it. That's not a separate signal; it's just rational sizing of the response.

## Capturing the wave and exiting

Riding a wave and selling "when it's done increasing" has a clean mapping to the filtered signal's behavior. You're long while the smoothed prediction (or its trend component) is positive and holding up. You begin scaling out when the trend flattens or rolls over — i.e., the derivative of the filtered signal crosses down — rather than waiting for the level itself to go negative, because by then you've given back much of the move. A trailing mechanism on the filtered conviction works well here: track the peak conviction since entry and exit when conviction retraces some fraction of that peak. This lets winners run while the wave builds and cuts them once momentum decays, which is precisely the "ride it up, sell when done" objective.

## Holding-period discipline

Because you're day-trading and don't want to over-hold, I'd impose a couple of soft constraints layered on top. A minimum holding period (or equivalently, a cap on turnover rate) prevents the algorithm from flickering even if some band gets misconfigured — this is a safety rail. A maximum holding period or end-of-day flatten enforces the day-trading mandate so nothing turns into an accidental long-term bet. These are blunt instruments and the hysteresis/filtering should be doing most of the work; the holding constraints just bound pathological behavior.

## Portfolio-level construction

Don't size each stock in isolation. The 500 stocks are correlated, and naive per-stock sizing will concentrate risk into whatever sector the robot currently likes. I'd run the conviction scores through a portfolio construction step that targets a risk budget — scaling gross exposure so total predicted portfolio volatility stays roughly constant, and damping positions in stocks that are highly correlated with positions you already hold. Even a simple volatility-scaling per name (bigger positions in lower-vol stocks for equal conviction) meaningfully improves the risk-adjusted ride. This also means that when the robot's signals across the book get noisier and less consistent, your overall filter uncertainty rises, your conviction scores compress toward zero, and your gross exposure automatically shrinks — the system de-risks itself in exactly the conditions where the robot is least trustworthy.

## Putting it together as a pipeline

The end-to-end flow each cycle: ingest the new 500-stock prediction vector; update the per-stock adaptive filter to get smoothed level, trend, and uncertainty; convert to uncertainty-adjusted conviction and rank cross-sectionally; map conviction to target positions through a saturating function with volatility scaling and correlation damping; compare targets to current holdings and only trade where the gap exceeds the cost-aware no-trade band, applying hysteresis on entries/exits and trailing-peak exits; enforce holding-period and end-of-day constraints. Every parameter — filter decay, threshold gap, band width, trailing fraction — is a knob on the same underlying responsiveness/stability dial, and the adaptive elements (noise-driven filter gain, uncertainty-driven exposure) let those knobs effectively self-adjust to how well-behaved the robot is at any moment.

One honest caveat: all of this should be validated on historical prediction streams with realistic cost and slippage assumptions before trusting it, because the optimal settings depend heavily on the actual statistical character of your specific robot's errors — their autocorrelation, how fast genuine waves move, and how predictive the signal really is once smoothed. The architecture is sound; the parameter values are an empirical question, and overfitting them to past data is the main way a design like this fails in practice.




This is from the same prompt but given to opus 4.6 instead of opus 4.7:

# Strategy for a Prediction-Driven Short-Term Trading Algorithm

## The Core Problem

You have a noisy oracle: every 3 minutes it hands you 500 return forecasts, but those forecasts jitter significantly between updates. If you naively rebalance to match each new prediction snapshot, you'll churn your portfolio every 3 minutes, transaction costs and slippage will eat you alive, and you'll never actually capture the moves the robot is identifying. The challenge is to extract the *signal* from the robot's predictions while filtering the *noise*, and to trade on that signal with appropriate conviction and timing.

## Signal Extraction: Smoothing the Predictions

The first layer is to stop treating each raw prediction as ground truth. Instead, for each stock, I'd maintain a running smoothed forecast using an exponentially weighted moving average (EWMA) of the robot's predictions over time. The decay parameter is critical — it controls how quickly you respond to new information versus how much you trust the accumulated history. A half-life of maybe 3 to 5 prediction cycles (roughly 9 to 15 minutes) is a reasonable starting point, meaning the robot needs to be *consistently* bullish on something across several updates before your smoothed signal fully reflects that conviction.

This accomplishes something important: if the robot says stock X will return +2% in one cycle, then -1% the next, then +1.5%, the smoothed signal picks up that there's a moderate positive tendency without whipsawing you in and out.

## Conviction Scoring: When Is a Signal Worth Acting On?

Beyond the smoothed level of the prediction, I'd track a second quantity: the *consistency* or stability of the robot's view on each stock. If the robot has been positive on stock X for the last 6 updates in a row, even if the magnitude varies, that's a much more actionable signal than a single large positive prediction that appeared out of nowhere. You can formalize this as the fraction of recent predictions that agree in sign, or as the inverse of the coefficient of variation of recent predictions, or simply as a ratio of the smoothed prediction to the standard deviation of recent raw predictions — essentially a signal-to-noise ratio for each stock.

A stock only becomes a candidate for entry when both the smoothed prediction exceeds some threshold *and* the consistency score is high enough. This naturally filters out the one-off noisy spikes.

## Position Sizing: Proportional to Confidence

When you do decide to hold a stock, the size of the position should reflect how strong and stable the signal is. A stock where the robot has been consistently and strongly bullish gets a larger allocation than one where the signal just barely crossed the threshold. This can be done by making position size proportional to something like the smoothed prediction multiplied by the consistency score, then normalizing across all candidates so you stay within your capital and risk constraints.

## Entry Logic: Don't Jump In Immediately

When a stock first starts showing a positive smoothed signal, I wouldn't buy immediately. I'd require a "confirmation window" — the signal needs to persist for some minimum number of cycles (say 2 to 4) before I enter. This is the patience tax that prevents you from reacting to noise. The cost is that you miss the very beginning of some moves, but the benefit is that you avoid a huge number of false starts.

## Exit Logic: This Is Where the Nuance Lives

Exits are harder than entries, and they need to be asymmetric. You want to ride winners but cut losers. A few mechanisms working together:

**Signal deterioration**: if the smoothed prediction for a stock you hold drops below some exit threshold (which should be lower than the entry threshold, creating a band of hysteresis so you're not flickering in and out), you start reducing the position. The hysteresis is crucial — your entry threshold might be a smoothed prediction of +0.5%, but you don't exit until it drops to, say, +0.1% or turns negative.

**Momentum decay detection**: even if the smoothed prediction is still positive, if it's been declining over the last several updates, that's a sign the move is running out of steam. You can track the slope of the smoothed prediction over recent history, and if it turns negative while the level is still positive, you start tightening your exit threshold or scaling down the position.

**Time-based decay**: since you're day-trading and don't want to hold too long, every position should have a soft clock running. The longer you hold something, the more you should be biased toward exiting. You can implement this as a gradually tightening exit threshold — a position you've held for 5 minutes gets a lot of latitude, but one you've held for 90 minutes needs a very strong continuing signal to justify keeping.

**Realized move accounting**: if a stock has already moved significantly in your favor since entry, lower the bar for exiting. You've captured a chunk of the wave; don't be greedy. Conversely, if it's moved against you, exit more aggressively — the robot was probably wrong on this one.

## Rebalancing Frequency and Trade Throttling

Even with all this smoothing, you don't want to execute trades every 3 minutes. I'd decouple the signal update cycle from the trade execution cycle. Update signals every time the robot makes a prediction, but only execute trades on a slightly slower cadence (say every 6 to 15 minutes), or only when the desired portfolio has drifted sufficiently far from the actual portfolio. Define a minimum trade size below which you don't bother — if the algorithm says to increase position X by 0.2%, just skip it.

Additionally, for each individual stock, impose a minimum holding period. Once you buy something, you're committed for at least some number of cycles (maybe 4 to 6, so 12 to 18 minutes) unless a hard stop-loss is triggered. This prevents the pathological case where smoothing and consistency scores temporarily aligned by chance for one cycle but immediately fell apart.

## Portfolio-Level Constraints

At the portfolio level, you want to control concentration and turnover. Cap the number of simultaneous positions (maybe 20 to 40 out of the 500), so you're only in the robot's highest-conviction picks. Cap daily turnover as a fraction of portfolio value. If you've already traded a lot today, raise the bar for new trades. This acts as a natural brake on overreaction.

## Adaptive Calibration

Finally, and this is important: periodically evaluate how well the robot's predictions actually perform. Track the correlation between the smoothed predictions and realized returns at various horizons. If the robot is better at predicting some stocks than others, weight its opinions accordingly — effectively learning stock-specific reliability scores. If the robot goes through a period of being especially noisy or especially accurate, you can adapt the EWMA decay rate and conviction thresholds dynamically. This is essentially an outer loop of Bayesian updating on the robot's skill level.

## Summary of the Philosophy

The whole strategy boils down to: treat the robot as a biased, noisy expert witness rather than an oracle. Don't do what it says right now — do what it has been *consistently saying* over a short recent window. Enter slowly, with confirmation. Exit asymmetrically, being quicker to cut losses and slower to abandon winners, but never holding anything so long that you're no longer day-trading. And always let the portfolio-level constraints act as a governor so that no single noisy prediction can cause catastrophic churn.
